dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for stab/gunshot wound/penetrating trauma.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
She was arguing with a friend and got stabbed in the stomach. She's in a lot of pain and feels weak.
I'm dizzy and in pain, particularly in my stomach.
Can you open your eyes for me?
What is your name?
What city are we in?
Can you point to the area where you feel the worst pain?
Your airway is clear and patent with no signs of obstruction.
Your breathing is even, at a rate of 16 breaths per minute.
I'll now take your blood pressure.
Your oxygen saturation is 97% and remains stable.
You appear pale and clammy, and I cannot find pulses in your extremities.
You seem a bit sluggish. Can you remain with me and answer questions?
I will now attach leads to monitor your heart's electrical activity.
Your glucose level is not showing on the device. We'll keep it in mind moving forward.
The EKG shows a fast heart rate, which is called sinus tachycardia, and no irregularities.
On a scale from 0 to 10, how would you rate your pain right now?
When did you first start feeling pain?
Is this pain staying in your stomach, or is it spreading?

Other than the pain, are you experiencing any other symptoms? 22. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: allergies; EMT: Are you allergic to any medications, food, or other substances? 23. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: medications; EMT: Are you taking any medications-prescribed or over-the-counter?
Have you ever had any surgeries or long-term medical conditions?
I'll check your skin and see that there are small cuts on your upper arm and a larger one in your upper belly.
These wounds are sealed with gauze and packing and are not actively bleeding at this time.
Your head and neck look normal with no visible injuries or swelling.
Your chest moves equally on both sides, and I can hear clear breath sounds.
Your abdomen is soft and not tender, and the wound is clean and packed.
Can you describe what you saw or what you believe was used for the stabbing?
I noted that the abdominal wound has already been packed with Quikclot and combat gauze.
Wounds on your upper arm are also secured with rolled gauze to apply pressure.
Your heart rate is up to 114 now, which is notably higher than previously.
Your breathing is still steady at 18 breaths per minute.
Your blood pressure now dropped to 0/0. This is serious and will require immediate attention.
Your oxygen level remains at 98% and is stabilized.
We're still unable to obtain a glucose reading.
The EKG shows your heart remains in a regular sinus rhythm.
Your GCS is 15-fully oriented and responsive.
I've started an IV in your right antecubital vein using an 18-gauge needle.
The IV flushed well with normal saline and is ready for use.
We will continue monitoring your heart with the four-lead EKG.
I have assessed your airway, bleeding is controlled, and I have taken your vitals.
Based on your condition, we'll transport you directly to VCU Medical Center for specialized care.
We are now preparing to move you to the stretcher in a semi-Fowler's position with two persons.
We have securely fastened the straps and you are safe for transport.
We will repeat your vital signs and recheck your wounds and pain levels during transport.
Your heart rate remains at 114 beats per minute.
Still 18 breaths per minute-no changes observed.
The EKG shows your heart rhythm is still regular and stable.
I verified the IV is intact and flowing without any issues.
Your mental status remains the same-somnolent but aware and answering.
I will also inspect the pressure dressing to ensure it does not shift during transport.
I continue to monitor your EKG and note the findings.
Your skin remains pale and clammy with no new injuries.
I have not observed any other penetrating injuries or objects in place.
Your pulse continues at 114 as we head to the hospital.
Still on 18 breaths-no deviations so far.
The EKG remains consistent with regular rhythm.
Upon arrival, we will hand you to the emergency team and give them a full report.
We will monitor your mental state, blood pressure, and overall condition during the ride.
I will assist you onto the hospital stretcher using a two-person sheet pull upon arrival.
Your heart rate is still at 114.
Still on 18 breaths-no changes at this time.
EKG remains in sinus rhythm-no new issues recorded.
We have completed our report and passed over your care to the emergency department team.
We will repeat your vital signs and evaluate your consciousness status one last time before arrival.
You are now safely transferred to the ED bed for immediate physician evaluation.
All critical information and treatments have been clearly communicated to the ED team.
All necessary actions were completed according to the hemorrhage control protocol.
We have noted all vital signs and interventions and ensured effective handover to the ED.
I will confirm with the hospital that they are aware of your condition upon arrival.
The transport was uneventful and followed all critical care procedures.
The EMS team is ready for the next call now that care was effectively transferred.
